title: SAA-C03 Daily Mock
subtitle: Day 1 — AWS Accounts, Root User, MFA & IAM Basics
topics_covered: AWS Accounts (account root user, multi-account structure)
IAM Basics (users, groups, roles, policies, least privilege, global service)

questions.stem: A new startup creates its first AWS account using a company email address, a password, and a credit card. Which statement BEST describes the identity that this email address and password now represent?

options.text: The account root user, which has full and unrestricted control over the entire account and cannot be limited by IAM permissions policies.
options.why: Correct — the email/password used to create an account is the account root user. It has complete control over that single account and its access cannot be restricted by IAM policies.

options.text: An IAM administrator user that can be deleted later to lock down the account.
options.why: Wrong — the root user is created with the account itself; it is not an IAM user and it cannot be deleted while the account exists.

options.text: A federated identity managed centrally by AWS Organizations.
options.why: Wrong — federation and Organizations are unrelated to how the root user is created, and a brand-new standalone account is not federated.

options.text: A service role that AWS assumes in order to bill the account.
options.why: Wrong — billing is tied to the account, not to a service role; the credentials represent a human-controlled root login.

questions.stem: Immediately after creating a brand-new AWS account, a solutions architect wants to follow security best practice for the root user. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.text: Enable MFA on the root user, then create a separate IAM identity for day-to-day administration and avoid using root.
options.why: Correct — securing root with MFA and then using a less-privileged IAM identity for everyday work is the recommended pattern.

options.text: Use the root user for all daily administrative tasks because it already has full permissions.
options.why: Wrong — root should be used only for the handful of tasks that strictly require it; using it daily is a major security risk.

options.text: Delete the root user as soon as an IAM administrator user has been created.
options.why: Wrong — the root user cannot be deleted; it exists for the life of the account.

options.text: Attach a restrictive IAM permissions policy to the root user to limit what it can do.
options.why: Wrong — the root user's access cannot be restricted by IAM policies; it always retains full control.

questions.stem: A company wants to ensure that a stolen password alone cannot be used to sign in to AWS. Which authentication factor does enabling MFA add on top of the existing password?

options.text: Something you have, such as a code generated by a virtual MFA app or hardware device.
options.why: Correct — a password is 'something you know'; MFA adds a second factor, typically 'something you have' like a time-based code from an app or device.

options.text: A second, longer password stored in a separate system.
options.why: Wrong — another password is still 'something you know' and does not add a distinct factor.

options.text: A network restriction that only allows sign-in from the office IP range.
options.why: Wrong — an IP restriction is a network control, not an authentication factor.

options.text: An extra security question that only the user knows the answer to.
options.why: Wrong — a security question is still 'something you know', so it is the same factor as the password.

questions.stem: An architect is enabling MFA on the AWS account root user and wants a free option that runs on a smartphone they already own. Which type of MFA device should they configure?

options.text: A virtual MFA device using an authenticator app installed on the smartphone.
options.why: Correct — a virtual MFA device is an authenticator app that generates time-based codes; it uses an existing phone and costs nothing.

options.text: A hardware TOTP key fob purchased and shipped from a vendor.
options.why: Wrong — a hardware token is a valid MFA type but is a physical purchase, not the free smartphone-app option described.

options.text: A FIDO2 USB security key plugged into the phone.
options.why: Wrong — a FIDO2 security key is also a physical device that must be bought, not the free app-based option.

options.text: A code that AWS sends by SMS text message to the phone number on file.
options.why: Wrong — SMS is not the recommended/standard root MFA mechanism here; the smartphone option being described is a virtual MFA authenticator app.

questions.stem: A solutions architect creates an IAM user while the console is set to the us-east-1 region. The team later operates resources in eu-west-1 and ap-southeast-2. In which regions will this IAM user be usable?

options.text: In all AWS regions, because IAM is a global service.
options.why: Correct — IAM is a global service; identities such as users, groups, and roles are not tied to a region.

options.text: Only in us-east-1, where the console happened to be set when it was created.
options.why: Wrong — IAM identities are not region-scoped, so the console region at creation time is irrelevant.

options.text: Only in regions that have been explicitly enabled for the account.
options.why: Wrong — enabling regions affects where you deploy resources, not the global reach of an IAM identity.

options.text: Only in whichever single region is selected at sign-in time.
options.why: Wrong — a user can operate across regions; IAM itself is global.

questions.stem: Within a single AWS account, what is a key difference between the account root user and a newly created IAM user?

options.text: The root user always has full access that cannot be restricted, whereas a new IAM user starts with no permissions until they are explicitly granted.
options.why: Correct — root is all-powerful and cannot be limited, while a new IAM identity has an implicit deny on everything until policies grant access.

options.text: The root user is global while an IAM user is region-specific.
options.why: Wrong — both relate to IAM, which is a global service; neither is region-specific.

options.text: An IAM user can never be granted administrator-level permissions.
options.why: Wrong — an IAM user can absolutely be granted administrator permissions via policy.

options.text: The root user and an IAM user become functionally identical once both have MFA enabled.
options.why: Wrong — MFA only affects sign-in; the root user retains unrestricted, irrevocable power that an IAM user does not have.

questions.stem: A company organizes its IAM users into groups such as 'Developers' and 'Admins' and attaches permissions policies to those groups. Which statement about IAM groups is correct?

options.text: A group is a container used to manage permissions for its member users; you cannot sign in as a group and it is not a true identity that can be referenced as a principal.
options.why: Correct — groups exist only to make permission management easier; they have no credentials, cannot be logged into, and are not principals.

options.text: You can log in to the AWS console directly as a group using shared group credentials.
options.why: Wrong — groups have no login or credentials; only users (and assumed roles) can authenticate.

options.text: A user can belong to only one IAM group at any given time.
options.why: Wrong — a user can be a member of multiple groups simultaneously.

options.text: Permissions policies cannot be attached to groups, only to individual users.
options.why: Wrong — attaching policies to groups is exactly how groups are used to grant permissions.

questions.stem: An application needs short-term permissions to interact with AWS services without having long-term access keys embedded in it. Which IAM identity is designed to be assumed in order to provide temporary credentials?

options.text: An IAM role.
options.why: Correct — roles are assumed by trusted entities and provide temporary, automatically rotated credentials, avoiding long-term keys.

options.text: An IAM user with a permanent set of access keys.
options.why: Wrong — IAM user access keys are long-term credentials, which is exactly what the scenario wants to avoid.

options.text: The account root user.
options.why: Wrong — the root user should never be used for application access and does not provide assumable temporary credentials.

options.text: An IAM group.
options.why: Wrong — a group is not an identity that can be assumed and has no credentials of its own.

questions.stem: Following the principle of least privilege, how should permissions be assigned to an IAM identity?

options.text: Grant only the permissions required to perform the intended task, and nothing more.
options.why: Correct — least privilege means granting the minimum permissions needed, reducing the blast radius if credentials are misused.

options.text: Grant full administrator access up front and remove permissions only if a problem appears.
options.why: Wrong — starting with full access violates least privilege and leaves the identity over-permissioned.

options.text: Grant the same permissions as the root user to avoid any access issues.
options.why: Wrong — mirroring root's unlimited power is the opposite of least privilege.

options.text: Grant no permissions and rely on an implicit allow for common actions.
options.why: Wrong — IAM has an implicit deny, not an implicit allow, so this identity would be unable to do anything useful.

questions.stem: The Animals4life team decides to use separate AWS accounts (for example a general account and a production account) instead of running everything in one account. Which TWO benefits does using separate accounts provide? (Select TWO.)

options.text: It creates a strong security and permissions boundary between different environments.
options.why: Correct — separate accounts isolate resources and permissions, so issues or access in one account do not automatically affect another.

options.text: It allows billing and cost to be separated and tracked per account.
options.why: Correct — each account has its own billing, making it easy to attribute and track costs per environment.

options.text: It removes the need to enable MFA on any of the accounts.
options.why: Wrong — each account still has its own root user that should be protected with MFA.

options.text: It makes IAM a region-specific service within each account.
options.why: Wrong — IAM is global regardless of how many accounts you use.

options.text: It automatically replicates all resources between the accounts.
options.why: Wrong — separate accounts are isolated; resources are not automatically replicated between them.

questions.stem: When an identity makes a request to AWS, it is first checked to confirm it is who it claims to be, and then checked to confirm whether it is allowed to perform the requested action. These two steps are known respectively as:

options.text: Authentication and then authorization.
options.why: Correct — authentication proves identity; authorization determines what that authenticated identity is permitted to do.

options.text: Authorization and then authentication.
options.why: Wrong — the order is reversed; you must establish who the identity is before evaluating what it can do.

options.text: Federation and then delegation.
options.why: Wrong — these are different IAM concepts and do not describe the identity-then-permission check.

options.text: Encryption and then validation.
options.why: Wrong — encryption protects data; it is not the identity/permission evaluation being described.

questions.stem: A security review of a newly created AWS account examines how the root user is protected. Which TWO actions represent recommended best practices for the account root user? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Enable MFA on the root user.
options.why: Correct — MFA on root is a fundamental safeguard against compromise of the most powerful identity in the account.

options.text: Use the root user only for the few tasks that strictly require it, and use a separate IAM identity for everyday work.
options.why: Correct — minimizing root usage and relying on a less-privileged IAM identity for daily tasks is best practice.

options.text: Share the root user credentials with the whole admin team for convenience.
options.why: Wrong — sharing root credentials destroys accountability and massively increases risk.

options.text: Create long-term access keys for the root user to use in automation scripts.
options.why: Wrong — root access keys are strongly discouraged; if needed, automation should use IAM roles or users, not root keys.

options.text: Attach a deny-all IAM policy to the root user to limit its power.
options.why: Wrong — root cannot be restricted by IAM policies, so this would have no effect.
